coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon nanodots as fluorescent probes for selective detection and quantification of Ferric(III) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Chin, Suk-Fun
Tan, Shao-Chien
Pang, Suh-Cem
Ng, Sing-Muk
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen (N) doped carbon dots (N-CDs) that showed blue fluorescence with quantum yield (QY) of 12.25% were synthesized by one step microwave irradiation of lysine in ortho-phosphoric acid at 1000 W for 5 min. The as-synthesized N-CDs were successfully explored as fluorescent probes for selective detection of ferric (Fe3+) ions in aqueous condition with a linear range from 0.2 to 5.0 mM and a detection limit of 0.074 mM ± 0.081 (S/N = 3). The N-CDs exhibited high selectivity towards the detection of Fe3+ ions even in the presence of interfering ions. The N-CDs also demonstrated the potential of practical application for determining of Fe3+ ions concentration in real samples with high recovery rate and low relative standard deviation error.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
One–pot microwave-assisted synthesis
fluorescence detection
Ferric ions (Fe3+)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of excitation wavelength of N-CDs solution
Optimization of N-CDs concentration and pH
Optimization of conditions for detection of Fe3+ ions
Effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs
Selectivity and sensitivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrograph of as-synthesized N-CDs. Inset shows the particles diameter distribution of the N-CDs.
UV-Vis spectra of (a) lysine and (b) N-CDs. Inset shows the blue fluorescence property of N-CDs solution under UV-lamp of 365 nm (right) and under visible light (left).
FT-IR spectra of (a) lysine and (b) N-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 320 to 450 nm with increment of 10 nm.
Effect of concentration of N-CDs (mg/mL) on the fluorescence intensity (A.U.).
Effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensity (A.U.) of N-CDs.
Effect of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

Variation of fluorescence intensity (A.U.) with the concentration of Fe3+ ions (mM) based on the peak fluorescence emission wavelength of 410 nm. Inset shows the Stern-Volmer relation line of F0/F against concentration of Fe3+ ions (mM).
Effect of different metal cations on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at fixed concentration of 5 mM.
Relative error of fluorescence quenching intensity for Fe3+ ions in the presence of other metal cations.
Percentage recovery of spiked Fe3+ ions using N-CDs for various water samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon nanodots as fluorescent probes for selective detection and quantification of Ferric(III) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Resource Science and Technology, Universiti Malaysia Sarawak, 94300 Kota Samarahan, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Resource Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen (N) doped carbon dots (N-CDs) that showed blue fluorescence with quantum yield (QY) of 12.25% were synthesized by one step microwave irradiation of lysine in ortho-phosphoric acid at 1000 W for 5 min. The as-synthesized N-CDs were successfully explored as fluorescent probes for selective detection of ferric (Fe3+) ions in aqueous condition with a linear range from 0.2 to 5.0 mM and a detection limit of 0.074 mM ± 0.081 (S/N = 3). The N-CDs exhibited high selectivity towards the detection of Fe3+ ions even in the presence of interfering ions. The N-CDs also demonstrated the potential of practical application for determining of Fe3+ ions concentration in real samples with high recovery rate and low relative standard deviation error.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-Dots) are a new class of nanoparticles consisting of mainly carbon with sizes of below 10 nm [1,2]. C-Dots were first discovered by Xu and his team in 2004 during their attempt to purify graphite to obtain single-walled carbon nanotube electrochemically [3]. C-Dots were becoming a popular topic of studies due to their low toxicity [4], low material cost [5], and excellent photoluminescence properties [6]. Studies were done on modifying the surface of C-dots with the objective of giving them a specific role [7]. In recent years, much progress has been achieved in which these modified C-dots were applied in various applications such as chemical sensor [8], biosensor [9], drug delivery [10], molecular scaffold for anti-microbial agents [11], and supercapacitors [12]. C-Dots had been shown to be able to detect various cations such as Sn2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ [5,8,13].

Detection of metal cations by C-Dots was often carried out using the fluorescence quenching method [14,15] or the electrochemical approach [16]. In a recent example, C-Dots were used as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions [17]. The fluorescence quenching behavior of Cu2+ ions on C-Dots was studied under pH 4 which was shown to emit maximum fluorescence due to the protonation of amino groups. The observed quenching of fluorescence upon addition of Cu2+ ions was explained as due to the inner filter effect [18]. Additionally, our previous study has shown the use of C-Dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions via electrochemical approach [8]. Through modifying the surface of Screen-Printed Electrode (SPCE) with C-Dots, the oxidation peak of cyclic voltammogram was observed to have increased. Upon reacting with Fe3+ ions, bonds were formed with the carboxylic group on the C-Dots and this has further enhanced the conductivity of the C-Dots due to the cationic nature of Fe3+ ion as a good conductor.

Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (N-CDs) were shown to have improved fluorescence emission due to the nitrogen or amine group on the surface as compared to C-Dots without modification [19–21]. Several strategies of one-pot synthesis of N-CDs without additional surface modifications were reported. For instance, Wu and his team synthesized N-CDs using bombyx mori silk which is a natural biopolymer that contains protein (Nitrogen source) via a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis route [22]. The as-synthesized N-CDs were shown to exhibit strong fluorescence and low toxicity. They later tested the stability of the fluorescence property of N-CDs in physiological conditions and confirmed the potential of N-CDs in bioimaging as the fluorescence property was extremely stable and did not suffer quenching effect from the surrounding. Similarly, Ji and his group synthesized N-CDs by hydrothermal method using only chitosan which contains the nitrogen group and distilled water. Due to its high biocompatibility, they immobilized glucose oxidase onto N-CDs and use the complex as a mean for the determination of glucose [23]. On the other hand, Zhang & Chen synthesized their N-CDs hydrothermally using ethylene glycol and folic acid as their main nitrogen source. The resulting N-CDs were characterized to emit strong fluorescence intensity at excitation wavelength 360 nm. Upon addition of Hg2+ ions into the system, fluorescence property of N-CDs was quenched, due to the conversion of spirolactam structure of –CONH- group into an open-ring amide induced by Hg2+ ions [24].

In this study, we report a simple, fast and aqueous based synthesis approach to prepare N-CDs by using lysine as the precursor material. Lysine, one types of amino acid was chosen as the precursor material due to its structure containing both primary amine (RNH2) group and carboxyl (COOH) group [25]. The COOH group was known to be able to bind to Fe3+ ions [8,26,27] whereas N group was known to be able to enhance the fluorescence property of the C-dots [19,20].
All chemicals were of reagent grade and were used without further purification. Ultrapure (UPW) water (18.2 MΩ, 25 °C) used for dilution was obtained from a ELGA UK Water Purifying System.

In a typical synthesis, 1.0 g of lysine was dissolved in 10 mL of UPW and 20 mL of ortho-phosphoric acid (85%) was added to the solution. The solution was heated in a microwave (1000 W) for around 5 min until it turned black [28]. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to room temperature and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was added to neutralize the pH. Absolute ethanol was added to precipitate sodium phosphate in order to remove the salt. The samples were purified by dialyzing using dialysis membrane (1 kD MWCO) against UPW for 24 h. The dialyzed samples were subsequently oven-dried and re-dispersed in UPW to form 1 mg/mL of N-CDs solution.

The morphology and particle sizes of the N-CDs were characterized by using a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL Model JEM 1230). UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 25). FT-IR spectra were recorded from KBr/sample pellets within the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Nicole iS10). Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian) set under fluorescent mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of excitation wavelength of N-CDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: The emission and excitation slit width was set at 10 nm. 1 mg/mL N-CDs were used and diluted 10 times using UPW. The spectrofluorometer was set to record the emission peaks with varied excitation wavelength from 320 to 450 nm to determine the optimized excitation wavelength having the highest emission intensity. The emission wavelengths were measured from 350 to 600 nm.

Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution with a quantum yield (QY) of 54% [29] was chosen as the reference. The QY of the as-synthesized N-CDs was calculated using the formula Φ = Φ ref
n 2
I
A
ref
n
ref
2
A
I
ref
[18] where Φref is the QY of known solvent, n is the refractive index of the solvent, I is the integrated photoluminescence intensity and A is the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N-CDs concentration and pH
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs stock solutions were diluted to different concentrations (0.0067–0.0274 mg/mL) and the fluorescence intensity of each concentration was determined. For the optimization of pH condition, pH of the buffer solutions was adjusted using NaOH and HCl to various pH within the pH range of 2–12 and was used to dilute N-CDs stock solution to study the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at each pH value was recorded.

Under optimized conditions, different concentration of Fe3+ ions (0.2 mM–5 mM) were added into 80 μl of 1 mg/mL N-CDs solution and diluted to 3 mL using buffer solution. The fluorescence intensity of each concentration was measured under the optimized conditions. The selectivity of N-CDs was evaluated by replacing Fe3+ ions with other metal cations (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Li+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+) at fixed concentration of 5 mM. The interference of other metal cations on the reaction between N-CDs and Fe3+ ions was investigated by mixing other metal cations (5 mM) into solutions containing N-CDs and Fe3+ ions (5 mM).

Tap water, mineral water (Blue Ice Natural Mineral Water) and reverse osmosis water were chosen and obtained from known source. The real samples were spiked with known amount of Fe3+ ions. N-CDs were added to the solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured.

N-CDs were successfully synthesized using a one-pot microwave heating method. Fig. 1 shows the TEM micrograph of the as-synthesized N-CDs. The N-CDs were shown to be in uniform spherical shape. The diameter size range of the N-CDs is within the range of 1–5 nm as depicted in the inset of Fig. 1. The morphological characteristics were identical to that of other studies [30]. Fig. 2 shows the UV spectra of the as-synthesized N-CDs and lysine. The UV spectrum of as-synthesized N-CDs (Fig. 2b) showed a peak at the wavelength of 260 nm which was typical of N-CDs [31]. The newly formed peak could be attributed to aromatic π-π transition bond of C=C [32]. As for the UV spectrum of lysine (Fig. 2a), there was no observable peak which indicated the absence of N-CDs. N-CDs solution was observed to be a clear light yellow solution under visible light and was shown to emit blue fluorescence while under direct UV lamp at 365 nm (inset Fig. 2).

According to other studies and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) library [33–35], lysine contains several functional groups (COOH and NH2) which may be used for the synthesis of N-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of lysine (Fig. 3 a) matched with the database of NIST and the National Center for Biotechnology Information.

As shown in Fig. 3b, an absorption peak belonged to the nitrogen (N) group was observed at 1440 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. This observation suggested the N group from lysine were doped on the N-CDs which has enhanced its fluorescence property. The N group, was suspected to be from the broken down amide group of lysine due to the energy received from microwave heating and formed bonds with the C-Dots. The absorption peak at 3241 cm−1 was attributed to O-H functional group and 1647 cm−1 suggested the presence of C=O group, which formed the carboxylic (COOH) group [36] and might be used for the binding of Fe3+ ions. The presence of C=C group can also be observed as the peak overlaps at 1647 cm−1 [32]. Other absorption peaks observed included the CH2 (2923 cm−1, 2851 cm−1), CH (1051 cm−1, 859 cm−1) groups on the surface of the N-CDs [36–38]. The QY of N-CDs was measured by comparing it with quinine sulfate. The obtained QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 12.25%, which was comparatively higher than other reported values of 5.42% and 10% [26,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for detection of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength of the optimum peak was determined from 320 to 450 nm with an interval of 10 nm for each excitation. The peak with highest fluorescence intensity peak was achieved at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm as shown in Fig. 4 with a peak intensity of 572.47 at emission wavelength 410 nm. In this study, the emission intensity was observed to show an excitation dependent red shift and the peak was also showed a significant reduction in the intensity. This is quite commonly reported as one of the C-Dots characteristics that were due to the varying in energy levels caused by the different surface states on the C-Dots. This was also observed in our previous work, with the excitation dependent trend started to be obvious when the excitation wavelength was set above 390 nm [26].

Different concentrations of N-CDs solution were used to obtain higher fluorescence intensity peak. Fig. 5 shows the effect of concentrations of N-CDs on the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity was observed to increase linearly with the concentration of N-CDs. An optimized fluorescence intensity of 915.55 was obtained at the N-CDs concentration of 0.0274 mg/mL. Higher concentration was not used as the intensity signal will over shoot the instrument scale that having a maximum reading of 1000 A.U.

The effect of pH value ranging from 2 to 12 on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 6 . It was observed that under basic condition, the fluorescent intensity was decreased which could be due to the reaction between C=O group of N-CDs and NaOH used for pH adjustment. However, a weak acidic condition at pH 4.66 was shown to enhance the fluorescence property of N-CDs which could be due to the presence of C=O group on the surface of as-synthesized N-CDs [5,40]. A fluorescence intensity of 981.73 was achieved under the optimized conditions of pH 4.66 using N-CDs at a concentration of 0.0274 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the effect of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs with the fluorescence peak was observed to reduce significantly upon addition of Fe3+ ions. The peak was observed to diminish as the concentration of Fe3+ ions was increased. This mechanism of quenching could be explained as electron transfer mechanism in which binding of the electrons onto the binding sites of N-CDs would prevent the electron transfer onto N-CDs, thus resulting in quenching of fluorescence [41]. Since the fluorescence intensity was recorded immediately after addition of Fe3+ ions into N-CDs solution (and no further reaction time needed), the observed quenching of fluorescence indicated rapid reaction between Fe3+ ions and N-CDs.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of fluorescence quenching upon addition of Fe3+ ions into N-CDs solution. Under optimized conditions, the peak fluorescence intensity (A.U.) was observed to increase inversely proportional with increasing Fe3+ ions. The inset shows a linear graph of the Stern-Volmer relationship based on equation
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ C ]
[26] where F0 and F equal to the fluorescence intensity recorded before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [C] is the concentration of Fe3+ ion measured. The linear regression equation obtained was
F 0
F
= − 0.297 + 3.264
[
Fe
3 +
]
with correlation regression R2 value of 0.999. The Stern-Volmer constant was calculated from the gradient of the curve with a value of 3.264 mM−1. The limit of detection as determined based on the linear regression equation was 0.074 mM ± 0.081 at a signal to noise ratio of 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the as-synthesized N-CDs was investigated by using other common metal cations in aqueous solution. The effect of each metal cations on the quenching of fluorescence of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 9 . These results showed that the quenching of fluorescence was the most significant for Fe3+ ions only whereas other metal cations did not show any obvious difference in intensity as compared to that of the control without cations. The effect of fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ ions was determined in the presence of other metal cations was shown in Table 1 . The highest relative percentage error of 10.37% was observed in the presence of Ag+ ions which might be due to the formation of insoluble silver chloride (AgCl) in acidic condition. All other metal cations were observed to give a very low relative error of below 4%. Both the selectivity and interference studies have shown that as-synthesized N-CDs were highly selective towards binding with Fe3+ ions in solution and without significant interferences by other metal cations. Fe3+ ions could selectively bind onto the surfaces of N-CDs due to the high affinity of Fe3+ ions towards the N and O groups on the surfaces of N-CDs, resulting in the formation of Fe3+-N-CDs complexes. One of the possible origins of CDs fluorescence could be attributed to the presence of surface defects or energy traps. The disruption of such surface energy traps by the formation of Fe3+-N-CDs complexes would disturb the initial fluorescence, thereby causing quenching to occur [14]. During this process, electrons of N-CDs in excited state would relax back to ground state through a non-radiation route by the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) pathway. These electrons were transferred into the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions that were in close proximity. Hence, no photon was being released during the relaxation process of electrons and this process was observed as quenching. Other groups have also postulated similar quenching mechanism for interactions between Fe3+ ions and CDs [42,43].

The potential application of N-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions was further studied using the real samples. As real samples might not contain Fe3+ ions, spiking was required. As shown in Table 2 , the measured values were relatively close to that of the spiked value with high recovery rates from 97% to 103%, and low RSD % error which ranged from 2.37% to 4.70%. This study showed that the as-synthesized N-CDs were potentially useful as fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions detection in environmental samples.

A fluorescent probe has been successfully fabricated based on N-CDs synthesized from lysine using the microwave-assisted method for the detection of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium. This one-pot synthesis method enabled the preparation of N-CDs containing both carboxylic (COOH) and nitrogen (N) functional group on the surfaces of C-Dots. A quantum yield of 12.25% was obtained, which was comparatively higher than that of other studies. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.074 mM ± 0.081 (S/N = 3). Besides, as-synthesized N-CDs was shown to be highly selective towards Fe3+ ions and low interferences by other metal cations. The potential application of N-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples was demonstrated.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by Ministry of Higher Education, Malaysia via the award of an Exploratory Research Grant [Grant no: EGRS/STG05(01)/1005/2013(02)]. MyBrain15 and Zamalah Graduate Scholarship (ESSU) was gratefully acknowledged.
